speaker: interviewer
text: Judy, are there any experiences that stand out in your mind from while you were at Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there are some. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One dates back to-- a lot of-- wait a minute. Let me say that a lot of my experiencing this period as a child has to do with being disillusioned in  humanity. Not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being really shook by things that went on sometimes between people, Jewish people. It is like you expect it from the Germans, but I had some childlike idea that all the Jews would band together against all the Germans, which was not the case. There was a lot of nationalism. The Dutch Jews and the German Jews didn't get along. The Dutch Jews, the Czechoslovakian Jews, the German Jews didn't get along in-- in-- in Theresienstadt. And somehow or other, that, in a way, I think, disappointed me more than the danger from without, which I had grown up with that, to expect that. The other came as a shock time and time again. I remember that I had a-- a little friend. Well, let-- let's call it a boyfriend. You know, when you're 11 years old, it's nothing, you know, serious. It was a male little friend. And he came to Theresienstadt on the same transport that we did with his brother. His parents had long disappeared. He already lived in an orphanage in-- in Westerbork. And at first, his brother was put on transport around the time that my father was, and then he was. And I remember that I brought him to the train. I wasn't allowed to-- I-- I-- I went with him as far as was allowed. When my father went, my mother didn't want me there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I brought-- his name was Gunther-- to the train, and there was a-- a friend of mine, a girlfriend, who came along. And when we got to the train, then Gunther said, take my food card, because that way you can stand in line twice. But of course, it was very forbidden.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I didn't have any pockets to hide it, and my friend, whose name I will not mention because she also survived, said, I'll carry it for you. I have a pocket. And I said, thanks, and I'll come tonight and I'll get it. And I went there in the evening, she said she lost it. And I said, you couldn't have. You don't lose food cards. And she says, I lost it. And then I went to her mother and I said, Mrs. So and So, she's lying to me. And the mother said, you know, she doesn't lie. And, I mean, I can swear they had that card. Now, that's the kind of thing that hurt me, you know, those kind of experiences. And-- and-- and I remember feelings about that. I don't remember feelings about hunger or anything like that, but I remember feelings around that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the feelings around that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's probably of being betrayed by-- from a source that you don't expect. I once got very angry with my mother. Not outwardly-- I was well brought up, and you didn't show this kind of stuff. I still don't show emotions too well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: She pointed out to me that lots of kids brought potatoes home. And I said, well, you know where they get them. They steal them from the kitchen. And my mother said, so? And I said, they steal them. And my mother said, is that so bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, this is the same mother who had brought me up as a-- a good child, you know, religious, good, moral, and all that. And she said, I really think it would be a good idea, and she actually set me up. Because in my ski pants, which were-- had elastic at the bottom, sort of poofy, bell bottom or whatever you call that, she made a slit in the pocket so that I could grab some potatoes and put them in the pocket. And they would roll down to the bottom, and I wouldn't look very suspicious. And I-- I remember doing this a couple times, and I was absolutely frightened out of my, you know, wits. But-- but but these were-- were things that made this period so abnormal, because all the norms that one grew up with, moral norms, seem to-- or not all but many of them, seem to have gone by the wayside. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was an incident [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where my mother-- this was after my father had left already-- she met a-- a rabbi that she knew from before the war, from Germany, who lived in Theresienstadt with his wife and who was among the more privileged. They had a privileged class in Theresienstadt. They had one, I think, probably in every camp. And they invited us for Shabbat, for Friday night, and we thought we were invited to their room. Well, we were not. We were invited to their door to watch them light the candles, and then we were dismissed. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember my mother's reaction. She was absolutely incensed. And-- and incidentally, my mother, after the war, really lost our religion. She practiced some of it out of form, but not out of intent. She kept a kosher home after the war, but it was for my uncle, not for herself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of these experiences, I think, are, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to me, are-- indicate much more of a suffering than the daily-- the food, the-- the cold, the heat, whatever. It's like episodes like that-- that-- that keep on coming up. And I-- I was really-- I was disappointed in my mother, who was a wonderful woman and practical. [LAUGHS] Could you describe a little bit about the hygiene in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had these long, like, troughs with cold water, and you sort of had to elbow your way to a faucet to do some washing. And somehow or other, to me, it didn't seem to be that important, and I really wonder how much I washed. I don't remember a toothbrush. I remember that, once I lived in the orphanage, I could see my mother only one day a week. And the camp was-- was just rampant with lice and fleas and stuff like that, and if you had lice, your hair got shorn. And my mother took pride in my hair. That must, to her-- that must have been, to her, her chance of mothering. Because every week that I saw her, that I had a chance to be with her, she would insist on going through my hair with a fine-toothed comb. And the hair was snarled and knotted, and it didn't feel good, and I dreaded it. I actually dreaded seeing my mother because that fine-toothed comb would come out. And one of the things that my mother afterwards said was her pride-- that she got me through the camp with my hair. I mean, she put big-- that-- that was her chance to show her mothering. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I never had lice, head lice. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You know, people do the best they can, and my mother did the best that she could. And to her, this was very important. I remember one shower. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Red Cross came to inspect the camp. And all of a sudden, the orphanage was going to be held up as a-- an example of how good the Germans were to the Jews. So we were moved to a house, and we were given rooms. And instead of three flights-- three-deckers, there were two. And there were-- there were no more, I think, than six in a room. And there was an actual shower, and we all took a turn and had a shower there. I remember icky soap. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Red Cross Commission and Germans inspected this house, and I remember sitting on my bunk and just looking down. I did-- I figured if I don't see them, they don't see me. You know how children sometimes think. And even at that age, I remember the black boots. And then they left. And when the Red Cross Commission left the camp, we were moved back and things went back to normal. During that time period that the Red Cross Commission was there we got different food. They also [SIGHS] said that we had the chance to purchase things. So they put up storefronts of clothing and shoes, and people would recognize their possessions in those windows of the stuff that we had to leave in the-- in the cattle cars when we came in. But you couldn't buy anything. They put sausages. I remember there was one storefront that was sausages hanging in the window, and we would take turns looking at them. I mean, this was all pretend. And then the minute that the Red Cross Commission left-- and they were-- they were-- how shall we say-- they appeared to be convinced that the Nazis were good to the Jews. And it seems to me almost ludicrous that they couldn't look past that, and maybe they didn't want to. I don't know whether they didn't want to look past it. But then after they left, everything went back to normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- but I do remember that shower. It was warm water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And otherwise, I-- I don't remember. I got a-- a feather quilt as a cover, and I'm sure it came from somebody who had died. I remember having a little pillow, and I had a-- you know, the mattresses were-- were straw-covered. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, stuff like changing sheets? There were no sheets to change. But to some-- that didn't bother me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, it-- it must have been extremely-- I mean, we must have been dirty like-- like alley kids. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But that can wash off again. I-- as I mentioned, I-- I had an aunt who explained my-- my like for colognes as trying to get a smell out of my nose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any smells from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. But it must have been awful. It really must have been awful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, I-- I-- I-- I can't fathom that, you know, for people who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did all the things one normally does and then not to have an opportunity. I take a shower every day now. You know, it must have been very unpleasant. But I don't remember that. And I don't have a sense of smell now at all, which is odd. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] camp-- toward the end, before liberation, the other camps were-- they sent, again, carloads of-- of prisoners from other camps to Theresienstadt in an attempt, I think, at the time, to escape that these camps were liberated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we-- we got these cattle cars now coming back. And I remember that my mother volunteered, after hours, to help unload, because I'm sure she was looking for my father. And I remember standing in a-- in a line, you know, like, around the road where-- where people who came off the trains, and just going after my work and just standing there and watching what came by. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a lot of them came by who couldn't walk, and they were dead. And my mother never knew this, that I did this. But I wanted to see my father too, and he didn't show up. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the people from the other camps came a typhus epidemic. And so they started putting the people who came in from these transports in separate barracks. They isolated a section of the camp. And then as my-- with my privilege of my card of being a messenger, I got into these-- into these barracks. And I-- I went from bed to bed, and I looked, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and it looked like some of the pictures that are in some of the books now. And then, somehow or other, my mother found out that I had gotten in there and that-- that I had used my privileged-- what do you call it-- job, and there was much stricter supervision after that from the orphanage about where I would spend my-- my free time. [SIGHS] But somehow or other, I-- you know, we both were looking for my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you went into those barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, the funny thing is I don't remember any women. I only remember men. And they were lying in beds in-- in-- in the typical three-tier beds. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tears. I didn't cry. I still don't cry much, very, very little. I found a-- a diary that I started in '47, and I described, when we came to America, that my girlfriend was crying. And I wrote in this diary, 'and I can't cry. Isn't that crazy?' [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It was just-- you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a horror picture, and I still see that. I still see the-- the carts that came off the train with people on them. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] maybe that's the kind of thing a child shouldn't see. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I can't say that much more about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other incidents you remember from Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No. My mother always kept a bread in reserve. She saved up enough to have one bread. And somehow or other, she used that one then and was able to replace it. There was always a new bread that she had in reserve, waiting for my father to come back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't-- I mean, I remember the Red Cross Commission. That stands out. And I-- I remember the-- the orphanage. That stands out. And there were always rumors going around about transports this way, transport that way. There was a rumor that they were building gas chambers that I remember hearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know whether I processed it. I really don't know whether I processed a lot of stuff that went on. You know, somehow or other, I have this picture of me going through this period like a zombie, and maybe I did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And actually, I mean, you know, I once went back after the war when the communists were still controlling Czechoslovakia. And I had-- I thought, I got to see whether this is something I have in my mind or whether this camp really existed. And I remember when I came there I felt like a zombie again. So it's a certain kind of closing off. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It-- it's-- it's definitely not a feeling period, except for some of these incidences, which, you know, sort of stand out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Red Cross Commission came before, because apparently there were rumors outside of the camp that they were going to do something with us or to us. And so the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Red Cross Commission came. And then came the Russians. And they moved us-- they moved the kids. They moved the orphanage to a safer place right in the-- smack in the middle of the camp. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were terrible stories about the Russians, what they all did to people. And I was 12 at that time and not very impressed nor very knowledgeable about sex or anything like that, about sexual issues. The Russians had bicycles, and they had candy. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember that I wanted to ride a bike. I hadn't ridden a bike for I don't know how long, you know, since the Germans took it away in Holland. And so there were a-- a bunch of Russians sitting in a park, and they had bicycles on the lawn. And I went up to them, and I pointed to the bike. And he pointed at the grass next to him, and I pointed at the bike. And it was like, OK, you can take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I rode this bike through the camp, pleased as punch. Some of my orphanage-- what do you call them-- sleep mates wanted a ride. And I said, no, I got this from a Russian, and I wouldn't let anybody on. And then I brought it back, and we, again, had this thing of I should sit down, and I said, uh-uh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back, and it was past my regular hours. And lo and behold, my mother is there. And she asked some of the kids where I was, and they said, with a Russian, or she understood, with a Russian on a bike. And she must have gone. So when-- by the time I came walking in there, I got hit by my mother, and that is the only time that I remember being hit by my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She-- she slapped me because she was so worried. And there I was, and I had done something that everybody was told never to do. You know, you don't get close to the Russians. It was almost like they were feared more than the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you know, I was watched more closely after that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then because there was a typhus epidemic in Theresienstadt, the whole camp was put on quarantine. You couldn't leave. And so for 14 days, we were fed barley. The Russians thought that was a cure, and we just got barley. It seemed like we got barley for breakfast, lunch, and dinner. And then, finally, the-- the epidemic slowed down. Because they didn't want this to spread all over Europe. And that's when we were allowed to start leaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been probably the end of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945, or middle of June. But it was well after the liberation of the camp. And then they allowed us to-- to leave, but it had to be in-- in a transport sort. And we were put on trucks. I remember I got some clothes at that time, by the way, from that rabbi. They came through with something concrete. I had a-- I remember a pair of pants, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] boy's pants, and I got a pair of shoes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we went on open trucks. And the first stop we made was to the Little Fortress, which was the-- the camp that they sent prisoners to-- political prisoners and people in-- in Theresienstadt that didn't behave. And it had a very ominous ring always, and-- and this was the first time that we actually saw this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we went back to Holland very, very slowly. Railroads were-- were destroyed, so we would be on a-- a-- a train for you know, a day, and then we'd go back on trucks. And I don't remember where we slept, but it took weeks to get back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any belongings with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my book. I don't remember anything else. And again, I don't remember hygiene. I do remember that we went out in the meadows for-- for toileting needs. But I don't remembered any hygiene. I do remember, at one point, we traveled in a cattle car, and this time the door was open. And the car moved very slowly, maybe because of the way the tracks were at the time. And we had enough room to have-- make ourselves a bed. And it was only-- it was children and mothers. I think that was all that was on the-- on the-- in this car. And we came to a station in Germany, and there were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sitting in this open doorway with my legs dangling out, and it was safe to do that because the car didn't move that fast. So we came into the station, and all of a sudden we were surrounded with German ex-soldiers, ex-German soldiers, whatever. And they wanted-- they were begging for food. And I got a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I lost it. I started kicking, scratching, and my mother had to pull me in so that I wouldn't be, you know, hurt in-- in return. And somebody said to my mother that you-- you-- in German, you shouldn't talk that much hatred into your child. And my mother said, I have never talked hatred into her. It comes with, you know, the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember emotions about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just about this-- this absolute uncontrollable fury. I mean, they had to take me kicking and screaming. It was just like I let it all out. I don't know. So--
